claims.claim_text: Argon2id is the preferred algorithm for password hashing in new systems
A bcrypt work factor of at least 12 is required for new deployments
Plaintext storage of passwords is prohibited with no exception process
Apply a secret pepper before hashing and store it in a hardware security module separate from the credential database
Argon2id is the preferred password hashing algorithm and runs without operational issues at recommended parameters
A bcrypt work factor of 10 is sufficient and higher values caused unacceptable login latency
Plaintext password storage is forbidden in all environments including fixtures and test databases
Upgrade legacy hashes by rehashing at the user's next successful login rather than by bulk migration
Hashed rather than plaintext password storage limited the blast radius of the database exfiltration
Per account rate limiting reduced successful credential stuffing attempts by 90 percent
Add continuous monitoring of authentication failure rates to the on call dashboard

sections.heading: Abuse Prevention
sections.text: Per account rate limiting is an effective control, the incident retrospective measured a 90 percent drop in successful credential stuffing attempts after it was deployed [S03-C002].

sections.heading: Hashing Algorithms
sections.text: Argon2id is the preferred password hashing algorithm for new systems, corroborated by both the standards review and production experience [S01-C001, S02-C001].

sections.heading: Key Protection
sections.text: The standards review recommends a secret pepper applied before hashing and held in a hardware security module separate from the credential database [S01-C004].

sections.heading: Migration
sections.text: Legacy hashes should be upgraded by rehashing at each user's next successful login rather than by bulk migration [S02-C004].

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.text: Authentication failure rates should be continuously monitored on the on call dashboard so credential stuffing campaigns are detected within minutes [S03-C003].

sections.heading: Storage Policy
sections.text: Plaintext password storage is prohibited in every environment, a position corroborated by the standards review, engineering practice and incident evidence [S01-C003, S02-C003, S03-C001].

sections.heading: Conflicts
sections.text: The standards review requires a bcrypt work factor of at least 12 while the engineering field notes report a work factor of 10 as sufficient on older hardware [S01-C002, S02-C002]. The disagreement is recorded here and remains unresolved in line with the merge rules.

sections.heading: Gaps
sections.text: No gaps were identified beyond the unresolved bcrypt work factor disagreement recorded in the Conflicts section.
